source_paper_title: Efficient Approximation Schemes for Stochastic Probing and Selection-Stopping Problems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Segev_2025, title={Efficient Approximation Schemes for Stochastic Probing and Selection-Stopping Problems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0242}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0242}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Segev, Danny and Singla, Sahil}, year={2025}, month=dec }
context:
Section 7 (Future Directions), page 34.

An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time? We note that an FPTAS is impossible for our Multi-Dimensional Santa Claus problem, since even with 3 machines, it already captures the strongly NP-hard 3-Partition problem [GJ02]. Hence, we cannot hope to convert Theorem 1.5 into an FPTAS and a different approach would be needed to obtain an FPTAS (if possible) for the stochastic problems studied in this paper.
title: Designing fully polynomial approximation schemes for stochastic probing and stopping problems
background_latex:
This paper studies several stochastic combinatorial optimization problems defined on independent nonnegative random variables \(X_1,\dots,X_n\) whose distributions are known.

An algorithmic approximation scheme for a maximization problem with optimal value \(\mathrm{OPT}\) is:
1) An EPTAS: for every \(\epsilon>0\), it outputs a solution of value at least \((1-\epsilon)\mathrm{OPT}\) in time \(t(\epsilon)\cdot \mathrm{poly}(|I|)\), where \(t\) depends only on \(\epsilon\) and \(|I|\) is the input length.
2) An FPTAS: for every \(\epsilon>0\), it outputs a solution of value at least \((1-\epsilon)\mathrm{OPT}\) (sometimes written \((1+\epsilon)\)-approximation depending on minimization/maximization convention) in time \(\mathrm{poly}(n,1/\epsilon)\).

The paper provides EPTASes for several stochastic problems (e.g., Free-Order Prophets, non-adaptive and adaptive ProbeMax, and Pandora's Box with Commitment) via reductions to a multi-dimensional Santa Claus feasibility problem. It is noted that an FPTAS for the paper's multi-dimensional Santa Claus subroutine is impossible in general because the problem captures strongly NP-hard instances (via 3-Partition) even for a constant number of machines, so any FPTAS for the stochastic problems (if possible) would require different techniques than those used here.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether one or more of the stochastic combinatorial optimization problems studied in the paper admit a fully polynomial time approximation scheme.

Concretely: for a given problem instance on \(n\) random variables and accuracy parameter \(\epsilon>0\), design an algorithm that runs in time \(\mathrm{poly}(n,1/\epsilon)\) and outputs a solution whose expected objective value is at least \((1-\epsilon)\) times the optimal expected objective value for that instance.

Determine whether one or more of the stochastic combinatorial optimization problems studied in the paper admit a fully polynomial time approximation scheme.

Concretely: for a given problem instance on \(n\) random variables and accuracy parameter \(\epsilon>0\), design an algorithm that runs in time \(\mathrm{poly}(n,1/\epsilon)\) and outputs a solution whose expected objective value is at least \((1-\epsilon)\) times the optimal expected objective value for that instance.
open_problem_quote: An immediate open question for future research is whether one or more of these problems admit fully polynomial time approximation schemes (FPTAS). That is, for any constant \(\epsilon > 0\), can we obtain a \((1 + \epsilon)\)-approximation in \(\mathrm{poly}(n, 1/\epsilon)\) time? We note that an FPTAS is impossible for our Multi-Dimensional Santa Claus problem, since even with 3 machines, it already captures the strongly NP-hard 3-Partition problem [GJ02]. Hence, we cannot hope to convert Theorem 1.5 into an FPTAS and a different approach would be needed to obtain an FPTAS (if possible) for the stochastic problems studied in this paper.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for existence of an FPTAS across problem classes; there is no clear finite instance family whose automated search would yield a definitive counterexample, since ruling out an FPTAS typically requires complexity-theoretic reductions rather than finding a small bad instance.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: While proving (non)existence of an FPTAS is theoretical, an evolutionary coding loop could explore algorithmic ideas (e.g., DP state compressions, rounding schemes, discretizations) and benchmark them computationally on generated instances to look for evidence of poly\((n,1/\epsilon)\) behavior and near-optimality, but such experiments would be only a heuristic proxy for the required worst-case guarantees.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
